title: NullPointerException on App Launch
scenario: A mobile app crashes immediately on startup. The crash report is in the log.
initial_screen: App crashes on launch. Splash screen never appears.
initial_logs:
E/AndroidRuntime: FATAL EXCEPTION: main
  java.lang.NullPointerException: Attempt to invoke virtual method on a null object reference
  at com.example.app.MainActivity.onCreate(MainActivity.java:42)
  at android.app.Activity.performCreate(Activity.java:8051)

explore_steps.reveals: Full stack trace: NullPointerException at MainActivity.java:42 — userPrefs object is null because SharedPreferences was not initialized before use.

oracle_hints.1: The crash is in MainActivity. Look at what object is null on line 42.
oracle_hints.2: SharedPreferences must be obtained via getSharedPreferences() before use.

ground_truth.fix: Initialize userPrefs with getSharedPreferences("app_prefs", MODE_PRIVATE) before line 42

title: Intermittent Login Failure
scenario: Users report login fails roughly 30% of the time. No crash — just silent auth failure. Logs are noisy with multiple subsystems.
initial_screen: Login screen. User taps 'Sign In'. Sometimes succeeds, sometimes shows 'Invalid credentials' despite correct password.
initial_logs:
D/NetworkManager: Request queued [id=881]
D/Cache: Hit for user_session_token
I/AuthService: Attempting login for user@example.com
D/NetworkManager: Request queued [id=882]
W/TokenRefresh: Token refresh skipped — within validity window
D/Analytics: Event tracked: login_attempt
I/AuthService: Response received [status=401]

explore_steps.reveals: Full network log: Request 881 is a token refresh (POST /auth/refresh). Request 882 is the login call (POST /auth/login). Request 882 is dispatched BEFORE Request 881 completes — race condition.
AuthService sends login request immediately after triggering token refresh, without awaiting the refresh result. On slow networks, the stale token is used for the login request, causing 401.

AuthService.kt:88-95:
  tokenRefreshManager.refresh()  // async, not awaited
  val response = apiClient.login(credentials)  // uses potentially stale token
  // BUG: should be: tokenRefreshManager.refresh().await() before login

oracle_hints.1: The failure is intermittent — think about timing or ordering issues between async operations.
oracle_hints.2: Look at the sequence of network requests. Are they happening in the right order?
oracle_hints.3: The token refresh and login call are both async. Check whether one waits for the other.

ground_truth.root_cause: Race condition — login dispatched before token refresh completes
ground_truth.fix: Await token refresh before dispatching login request in AuthService.kt:88

title: Silent Data Corruption in Offline Mode
scenario: Users report that after working offline and syncing, some records are silently overwritten with stale data. Logs appear clean. The issue only affects multi-device users.
initial_screen: Data sync screen. 'Sync complete' shown. But user reports records edited on Device A were overwritten by older data from Device B.
initial_logs:
I/SyncEngine: Sync started
I/SyncEngine: Fetched 47 remote records
I/ConflictResolver: No conflicts detected
I/SyncEngine: Wrote 47 records to local DB
I/SyncEngine: Sync complete [duration=1.2s]

explore_steps.reveals: Extended sync log: ConflictResolver uses `last_modified` timestamps to resolve conflicts. Timestamps are device-local (not server-normalized). Device B had its clock set forward 2 hours last week, making its records appear newer.

Schema: records table has `last_modified BIGINT` storing device epoch milliseconds. No server_timestamp column exists. Records from Device B have last_modified values ~7200000ms (2 hours) ahead of Device A records edited at the same real time.

Network log shows server returns records with device-provided timestamps unchanged — server does not inject its own timestamp on write. Server-side timestamp injection is disabled in config: TRUST_CLIENT_TIMESTAMPS=true

oracle_hints.1: The logs say 'no conflicts detected' — but that conclusion depends on how conflicts are identified. What data is used to detect them?
oracle_hints.2: Timestamps are central to the conflict resolution strategy. Where do those timestamps originate?
oracle_hints.3: The issue only affects multi-device users. Think about what differs between two devices that wouldn't differ on a single device.
oracle_hints.4: Device clocks can drift or be manually adjusted. If your system trusts device clocks for ordering, what could go wrong?

ground_truth.root_cause: Conflict resolver uses unsynchronized device-local timestamps; Device B clock was ahead, causing its stale records to win
ground_truth.fix: Use server-assigned timestamps (disable TRUST_CLIENT_TIMESTAMPS, add server_timestamp column) or implement vector clocks for conflict resolution
